_meta.dataPolicy: WSZYSTKIE dane SYNTETYCZNE (przyklad.test, NotkaApp/SklepDemo/PipeDemo, identyfikatory testowe, PESEL placeholder 00000000000). ZERO realnych danych klienta/PII.
_meta.note: Pytania modułu M8. Metadane z master allocation (rozkład globalny). Plik <800 LOC (AGENTS).

questions.learningOutcome: The participant analyzes an LLM-generated set of test cases and identifies a coverage gap relative to a requirement.
questions.prompt: Requirement for the NotkaApp application (fictional): a user resets their password via an email link valid for 30 minutes; after 3 failed login attempts the account is locked for 15 minutes. The LLM generated the cases: TC1 — successful reset; TC2 — link expired after 30 min; TC3 — 3 wrong passwords -> account locked. Which coverage gap is the most important one to fill here?

options.text: Missing case for reusing the same reset link (a single-use link used twice)
Missing case checking the color of the Reset password button
Missing case with an exactly 30-minute administrator session duration
Missing case verifying the application logo on the login page

questions.feedbackCorrect: A reset link is inherently single-use and time-limited, so the lack of a test for its reuse is a real security gap in the coverage of the requirement.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: The coverage gap concerns behavior aligned with the requirement (the link's validity and single-use nature), not cosmetic elements. Go back to the screen on coverage gaps.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant recognizes a sham case in an LLM output (an assertion that verifies nothing) and distinguishes it from a valuable test.
questions.prompt: In the set of cases for NotkaApp you find: TC4 — 'Open the password reset form and check that the form opens; expected result: none'. How should TC4 be classified?

options.text: Sham test — no defined expected result, the assertion actually checks nothing
A valid negative case for the account lockout scenario
A valid boundary case for the 30-minute link validity
A duplicate of case TC1 successful reset, which can be merged right away

questions.feedbackCorrect: Without a defined expected result the test will always 'pass' and verifies nothing — that is the definition of a sham test.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: The key is the missing expected result, not the scenario type or similarity to another TC. Review the screen on duplicates and sham tests.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant assesses whether a generated LLM output is suitable for QA documentation, identifying a fabricated fact that contradicts the requirement.
questions.prompt: The LLM generated a case description for NotkaApp with the note: 'After 3 failed attempts the account is locked for 24 hours, and the reset link is valid indefinitely'. The requirement states: lockout 15 minutes, link valid for 30 minutes. What is the most appropriate content-review decision before pasting this into the documentation?

options.text: Reject the fragment — it contains facts that contradict the requirement (fabricated values of 24 h and indefinitely), it needs correction before use
Accept it as is — the LLM usually provides values closer to reality than the requirement
Accept it and add a note that the values may vary depending on the application version
Reject the entire password reset test module, because the output contains one error

questions.feedbackCorrect: Output that contradicts the requirement contains fabricated facts (hallucinations) and cannot enter the documentation without correction — content review ends with a 'fix/reject the fragment' decision.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: The requirement is the source of truth, not the LLM output; nor should the entire work be rejected because of a single fragment. Go back to the accept/fix/reject rubric.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant defines the boundaries of human-in-the-loop, identifying a QA task in which the LLM remains an assistant while a human makes the decisions.
questions.prompt: A QA team wants to use an LLM for NotkaApp testing. Which proposal best captures the 'assistant, not decision-maker' principle?

options.text: The LLM generates a draft of test cases and a risk checklist, while the tester approves the test scope and decides on release approval
The LLM independently approves the release to production if the generated tests pass
The LLM ultimately assesses critical risk and closes defects without the tester's involvement
The tester manually rewrites every case, and the LLM is not used at any stage

questions.feedbackCorrect: The LLM speeds up creating drafts and proposals, but decisions about acceptance, critical risk and release belong to the human (human-in-the-loop).
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Decisions about release and critical risk assessment cannot be handed over to the LLM, but giving up the assistant entirely wastes its value. Review the assistant vs. decision-maker screen.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant identifies categories of coverage gaps that an LLM often does not generate without an explicit instruction.
questions.prompt: You are generating test cases for the password reset feature in NotkaApp. Which categories are most often omitted by the LLM and require deliberate completion? Select all that apply.

options.text: Boundary cases (e.g., a link used exactly in the 30th minute)
Error paths and exceptional states (e.g., a link used a second time, concurrent sessions)
Happy path of the password reset with valid data
Test data for extreme values (e.g., an email of maximum length)
The browser window title on the confirmation page

questions.feedbackCorrect: The LLM usually covers the happy path well, while omitting boundary cases, error paths and extreme data — that is exactly where we look for coverage gaps.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: The happy path is usually covered, and the window title is a cosmetic detail; the gaps concern boundaries, error paths and extreme data. Go back to the screen on coverage gaps.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant recognizes the characteristics of a duplicate and a sham test in a generated set of cases.
questions.prompt: You are reviewing a generated set of cases for NotkaApp for quality. Which statements about duplicates and sham tests are true? Select all that apply.

options.text: Two cases checking the same conditions with a different description are a duplicate, despite the different wording
A case without an expected result is a sham, because its assertion verifies nothing
A larger number of generated cases always means better coverage
A test with a 'true == true' assertion unrelated to the feature is a sham
A duplicate is harmless, because two identical tests increase confidence in the result

questions.feedbackCorrect: A duplicate is the same conditions described differently, and a sham test has an assertion that verifies nothing; the number of cases is not a measure of coverage.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: A larger number of tests does not equal better coverage, and a duplicate increases maintenance cost without value. Review the screen on duplicates and sham tests.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant applies the content-review criteria that an LLM output must meet to enter the QA documentation.
questions.prompt: Before you paste an LLM-generated set of cases into the official QA documentation of SklepDemo (fictional), which content-review criteria must be met? Select all that apply.

options.text: Consistency with the requirement (no values that contradict the acceptance criteria)
No fabricated facts (hallucinations) and verifiability of the content
No sensitive data and no real PII in the examples
The output should be as long as possible, because greater volume signals quality
The output should be accepted solely because it was generated by a newer model

questions.feedbackCorrect: Admission to the documentation requires consistency with the requirement, no hallucinations, verifiability and no sensitive data — volume and model version are not criteria.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Text length and model version are not quality criteria; what matters is consistency, verifiability and the absence of sensitive data. Go back to the content-review rubric.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant distinguishes QA tasks in which the LLM is an assistant from decisions reserved for a human.
questions.prompt: Which QA tasks can be safely entrusted to an LLM as an assistant (with later human review)? Select all that apply.

options.text: A draft of test cases from the requirement as a starting point
A proposal of risk categories for a risk-based testing checklist
Rephrasing and organizing existing test descriptions
The final decision on approving a release to production
Independently closing a critical defect without tester verification

questions.feedbackCorrect: Drafts, proposals of risk categories and organizing content are the assistant's work; decisions about release and critical defects belong to the human.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: The decision about release and closing critical defects is the role of the decision-maker (the human), not the LLM. Review the assistant vs. decision-maker screen.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant assigns NotkaApp functionality to the appropriate risk category in a risk-based testing checklist.
questions.prompt: Match a NotkaApp feature or artifact to the most appropriate risk category in a risk-based testing checklist.

pairs.left: Account lockout after 3 failed login attempts
Storing the user's email in the database
pairs.right: Data (protection of personal data and confidentiality)

pairs.left: Reset link generation time under heavy load
Handling the reset form with a screen reader

questions.feedbackCorrect: Each feature goes into the risk category that best describes its main threat — that is the core of a risk-based testing checklist.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Categories get confused when you look at the technology instead of the feature's main threat (security, data, performance, accessibility). Review the screen on the risk checklist.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant assigns a typical defect of generated test cases to its consequence in QA.
questions.prompt: Match a typical defect of LLM-generated test cases for a SklepDemo requirement (fictional) to its main consequence in QA.

pairs.left: A generated case without an expected result
pairs.right: Sham test — always passes, does not detect a defect

pairs.left: Two cases with the same steps and data
pairs.right: Duplicate — bloating the set without new coverage

pairs.left: A case checking a field that is not in the requirement
pairs.right: Scope hallucination — a test beyond the requirement

pairs.left: Missing case for an error path described in the requirement
pairs.right: Coverage gap — an untested scenario from the requirement

questions.feedbackCorrect: Each defect has a different consequence for the set's quality: a sham test does not detect errors, a duplicate adds no coverage, a scope hallucination tests something outside the requirement, and a coverage gap leaves a risk untested.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Distinguish defects by their effect: no expected result = sham test, same steps = duplicate, a field outside the requirement = scope hallucination, a missing error path = coverage gap. Review the QA workbench screen on gaps and sham tests.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant assigns a specific risk from a NotkaApp feature to the test scenario that verifies it.
questions.prompt: Match a risk related to the NotkaApp password reset feature to the test case that verifies it.

pairs.left: Risk: the reset link works after expiration
pairs.right: Test: attempt to use the link after 30 minutes have passed

pairs.left: Risk: a single-use link works multiple times
pairs.right: Test: using the same reset link twice

pairs.left: Risk: the account does not lock after failed attempts
pairs.right: Test: 3 consecutive failed login attempts and a check of the lockout

pairs.left: Risk: reset works for a non-existent account
pairs.right: Test: requesting a reset for an unregistered email

questions.feedbackCorrect: Each risk is verified by a case that directly attempts to trigger the undesired behavior — this links risk-based testing with coverage.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: The test must target exactly the threat described by the risk (e.g., reusing the link, no lockout). Review the screen on the risk checklist and coverage gaps.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant assigns a quality problem in a generated output to the appropriate corrective action by the tester.
questions.prompt: Match a detected problem in an LLM-generated QA artifact to the appropriate corrective action.

pairs.left: Two cases check the same conditions with a different description
pairs.right: Merge the duplicates and keep one case

pairs.left: A case without a defined expected result
pairs.right: Remove or complete the assertion (sham test)

pairs.left: Missing case for account lockout after 3 attempts
pairs.right: Add a case covering the coverage gap

pairs.left: The output states a lockout of 24 h instead of 15 min
pairs.right: Fix the fact that contradicts the requirement before use

questions.feedbackCorrect: Each type of problem in the output has a corresponding action: merging a duplicate, fixing a sham test, filling a gap, or correcting a fabricated fact.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: The corrective action depends on the type of problem — do not confuse a duplicate with a coverage gap or a sham test with a hallucination. Review the screens on duplicates and output review.
